127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
130	
131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
132	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
133	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
134	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
135	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
136	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
137	
138	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
139

141	
142	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
143	
144	**The hard rule, explicit:**
145	
146	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.

154	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.

156	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. 23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.

26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.

79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. 92

94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.

32	\section{{\v C}ech cohomology of quasi-coherent sheaves}
33	\label{section-cech-quasi-coherent}
34	
35	\noindent
36	Let $X$ be a scheme.
37	Let $U \subset X$ be an affine open.
38	Recall that a {\it standard open covering} of $U$ is a covering
39	of the form $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$
40	where $f_1, \ldots, f_n \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ generate
41	the unit ideal, see
42	Schemes, Definition \ref{schemes-definition-standard-covering}.
43	
44	\begin{lemma}
45	\label{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}
46	Let $X$ be a scheme.
47	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
48	Let $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$ be a standard
49	open covering of an affine open of $X$.
50	Then $\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for
51	all $p > 0$.
52	\end{lemma}
53	
54	\begin{proof}
55	Write $U = \Spec(A)$ for some ring $A$.
56	In other words, $f_1, \ldots, f_n$ are elements of $A$
57	which generate the unit ideal of $A$.
58	Write $\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}$ for some $A$-module $M$.
59	Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
60	$\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
61	is identified with the complex
62	$$
63	\prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
64	\prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
65	\prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
66	\ldots
67	$$
68	We are asked to show that the extended complex
69	\begin{equation}
70	\label{equation-extended}
71	0 \to
72	M \to
73	\prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
74	\prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
75	\prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
76	\ldots
77	\end{equation}
78	(whose truncation we have studied in
79	Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is exact.
80	It suffices to show that (\ref{equation-extended})
81	is exact after localizing at a prime $\mathfrak p$, see
82	Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-characterize-zero-local}.
83	In fact we will show that the extended complex localized
84	at $\mathfrak p$ is homotopic to zero.
85	
86	\medskip\noindent
87	There exists an index $i$ such that $f_i \not \in \mathfrak p$.
88	Choose and fix such an element $i_{\text{fix}}$. Note that
89	$M_{f_{i_{\text{fix}}}, \mathfrak p} = M_{\mathfrak p}$. Similarly
90	for a localization at a product $f_{i_0} \ldots f_{i_p}$ and $\mathfrak p$
91	we can drop any $f_{i_j}$ for which $i_j = i_{\text{fix}}$.
92	Let us define a homotopy
93	$$
94	h :
95	\prod\nolimits_{i_0 \ldots i_{p + 1}}
96	M_{f_{i_0} \ldots f_{i_{p + 1}}, \mathfrak p}
97	\longrightarrow
98	\prod\nolimits_{i_0 \ldots i_p}
99	M_{f_{i_0} \ldots f_{i_p}, \mathfrak p}
100	$$
101	by the rule
102	$$
103	h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_{\text{fix}} i_0 \ldots i_p}
104	$$
105	(This is ``dual'' to the homotopy in the proof of
106	Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-homology-complex}.)
107	In other words, $h : \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}, \mathfrak p} \to M_\mathfrak p$
108	is projection onto the factor
109	$M_{f_{i_{\text{fix}}}, \mathfrak p} = M_{\mathfrak p}$ and in general
110	the map $h$ equal projection onto the factors
111	$M_{f_{i_{\text{fix}}} f_{i_1} \ldots f_{i_{p + 1}}, \mathfrak p}
112	= M_{f_{i_1} \ldots f_{i_{p + 1}}, \mathfrak p}$. We compute
113	\begin{align*}
114	(dh + hd)(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p}
115	& =
116	\sum\nolimits_{j = 0}^p
117	(-1)^j
118	h(s)_{i_0 \ldots \hat i_j \ldots i_p}
119	+
120	d(s)_{i_{\text{fix}} i_0 \ldots i_p}\\
121	& =
122	\sum\nolimits_{j = 0}^p
123	(-1)^j
124	s_{i_{\text{fix}} i_0 \ldots \hat i_j \ldots i_p}
125	+
126	s_{i_0 \ldots i_p}
127	+
128	\sum\nolimits_{j = 0}^p
129	(-1)^{j + 1}
130	s_{i_{\text{fix}} i_0 \ldots \hat i_j \ldots i_p} \\
131	& =
132	s_{i_0 \ldots i_p}
133	\end{align*}
134	This proves the identity map is homotopic to zero as desired.
135	\end{proof}
136	
137	\noindent
138	The following lemma says in particular that for any affine scheme
139	$X$ and any quasi-coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ on $X$ we have
140	$$
141	H^p(X, \mathcal{F}) = 0
142	$$
143	for all $p > 0$.
144	
145	\begin{lemma}
146	\label{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}
147	\begin{slogan}
148	Serre vanishing: Higher cohomology vanishes on affine schemes
149	for quasi-coherent modules.
150	\end{slogan}
151	Let $X$ be a scheme.
152	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
153	For any affine open $U \subset X$ we have
154	$H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for all $p > 0$.
155	\end{lemma}
156	
157	\begin{proof}
158	We are going to apply
159	Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-vanish-basis}.
160	As our basis $\mathcal{B}$ for the topology of $X$ we are going to use
161	the affine opens of $X$.
162	As our set $\text{Cov}$ of open coverings we are going to use the standard
163	open coverings of affine opens of $X$.
164	Next we check that conditions (1), (2) and (3) of
165	Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-vanish-basis}
166	hold. Note that the intersection of standard opens in an affine is
167	another standard open. Hence property (1) holds.
168	The coverings form a cofinal system of open coverings of any element
169	of $\mathcal{B}$, see
170	Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-standard-open}.
171	Hence (2) holds.
172	Finally, condition (3) of the lemma follows from
173	Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}.
174	\end{proof}
175	
176	\noindent
177	Here is a relative version of the vanishing of cohomology of quasi-coherent
178	sheaves on affines.
179	
180	\begin{lemma}
181	\label{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing}
182	Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
183	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
184	If $f$ is affine then $R^if_*\mathcal{F} = 0$ for all $i > 0$.
185	\end{lemma}
186	
187	\begin{proof}
188	According to
189	Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
190	the sheaf
191	$R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
192	$V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$.
193	By assumption, whenever $V$ is affine we have that $f^{-1}(V)$ is
194	affine, see Morphisms, Definition \ref{morphisms-definition-affine}.
195	By Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero} we conclude that
196	$H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}) = 0$
197	whenever $V$ is affine. Since $S$ has a basis consisting of affine
198	opens we win.
199	\end{proof}
200	
201	\begin{lemma}
202	\label{lemma-relative-affine-cohomology}
203	Let $f : X \to S$ be an affine morphism of schemes.
204	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
205	Then $H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = H^i(S, f_*\mathcal{F})$ for all $i \geq 0$.
206	\end{lemma}
207	
208	\begin{proof}
209	Follows from Lemma \ref{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing}
210	and the Leray spectral sequence. See
211	Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-apply-Leray}.
212	\end{proof}
213	
214	\noindent
215	The following two lemmas explain when {\v C}ech cohomology
216	can be used to compute cohomology of quasi-coherent modules.
217	
218	\begin{lemma}
219	\label{lemma-affine-diagonal}
220	Let $X$ be a scheme. The following are equivalent
221	\begin{enumerate}
222	\item $X$ has affine diagonal $\Delta : X \to X \times X$,
223	\item for $U, V \subset X$ affine open, the intersection
224	$U \cap V$ is affine, and
225	\item there exists an open covering $\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$
226	such that $U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}$ is affine open for all $p \ge 0$ and all
227	$i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I$.
228	\end{enumerate}
229	In particular this holds if $X$ is separated.
230	\end{lemma}
231	
232	\begin{proof}
233	Assume $X$ has affine diagonal. Let $U, V \subset X$ be affine opens.
234	Then $U \cap V = \Delta^{-1}(U \times V)$ is affine. Thus (2) holds.
235	It is immediate that (2) implies (3). Conversely, if there is a
236	covering of $X$ as in (3), then $X \times X = \bigcup U_i \times U_{i'}$
237	is an affine open covering, and we see that
238	$\Delta^{-1}(U_i \times U_{i'}) = U_i \cap U_{i'}$
239	is affine. Then $\Delta$ is an affine morphism by
240	Morphisms, Lemma \ref{morphisms-lemma-characterize-affine}.
241	The final assertion follows from Schemes, Lemma
242	\ref{schemes-lemma-characterize-separated}.
243	\end{proof}
244	
245	\begin{lemma}
246	\label{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent}
247	Let $X$ be a scheme.
248	Let $\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$ be an open covering such that
249	$U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}$ is affine open for all $p \ge 0$ and all
250	$i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I$.
251	In this case for any quasi-coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ we have
252	$$
253	\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F})
254	$$
255	as $\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$-modules for all $p$.
256	\end{lemma}
257	
258	\begin{proof}
259	In view of
260	Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}
261	this is a special case of
262	Cohomology, Lemma
263	\ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-spectral-sequence-application}.
264	\end{proof}
265	
266	
267	
268	
269	
270	
271	
272	\section{Vanishing of cohomology}
273	\label{section-vanishing}
274	
275	\noindent
276	We have seen that on an affine scheme the higher cohomology groups
277	of any quasi-coherent sheaf vanish
278	(Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}).
279	It turns out that this also
280	characterizes affine schemes. We give two versions.
281	
282	\begin{lemma}
283	\label{lemma-quasi-compact-h1-zero-covering}
284	\begin{reference}
285	\cite{Serre-criterion}, \cite[II, Theorem 5.2.1 (d') and IV (1.7.17)]{EGA}
286	\end{reference}
287	\begin{slogan}
288	Serre's criterion for affineness.
289	\end{slogan}
290	Let $X$ be a scheme.
291	Assume that
292	\begin{enumerate}
293	\item $X$ is quasi-compact,
294	\item for every quasi-coherent sheaf of ideals
295	$\mathcal{I} \subset \mathcal{O}_X$ we have $H^1(X, \mathcal{I}) = 0$.
296	\end{enumerate}
297	Then $X$ is affine.
298	\end{lemma}
299	
300	\begin{proof}
301	Let $x \in X$ be a closed point. Let $U \subset X$ be an affine open
302	neighbourhood of $x$. Write $U = \Spec(A)$ and let
303	$\mathfrak m \subset A$ be the maximal ideal corresponding to $x$.
304	Set $Z = X \setminus U$ and $Z' = Z \cup \{x\}$.
305	By Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-reduced-closed-subscheme} there
306	are quasi-coherent sheaves of ideals
Now the higher direct images and base change sections.

545	\section{Quasi-coherence of higher direct images}
546	\label{section-quasi-coherence}
547	
548	\noindent
549	We have seen that the higher cohomology groups of a quasi-coherent module on
550	an affine are zero. For (quasi-)separated quasi-compact schemes $X$ this implies
551	vanishing of cohomology groups of quasi-coherent sheaves beyond a certain
552	degree. However, it may not be the case that $X$ has finite cohomological
553	dimension, because that is defined in terms of vanishing of cohomology
554	of {\it all} $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
555	
556	\begin{lemma}[Induction Principle]
557	\label{lemma-induction-principle}
558	\begin{reference}
559	\cite[Proposition 3.3.1]{BvdB}
560	\end{reference}
561	Let $X$ be a quasi-compact and quasi-separated scheme. Let $P$ be a property
562	of the quasi-compact opens of $X$. Assume that
563	\begin{enumerate}
564	\item $P$ holds for every affine open of $X$,
565	\item if $U$ is quasi-compact open, $V$ affine open,
566	$P$ holds for $U$, $V$, and $U \cap V$, then
567	$P$ holds for $U \cup V$.
568	\end{enumerate}
569	Then $P$ holds for every quasi-compact open of $X$
570	and in particular for $X$.
571	\end{lemma}
572	
573	\begin{proof}
574	First we argue by induction that $P$ holds for {\it separated} quasi-compact
575	opens $W \subset X$. Namely, such an open can be written as
576	$W = U_1 \cup \ldots \cup U_n$ and we can do induction on $n$ using
577	property (2) with $U = U_1 \cup \ldots \cup U_{n - 1}$ and $V = U_n$.
578	This is allowed because
579	$U \cap V = (U_1 \cap U_n) \cup \ldots \cup (U_{n - 1} \cap U_n)$
580	is also a union of $n - 1$ affine open subschemes by
581	Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-characterize-separated}
582	applied to the affine opens $U_i$ and $U_n$ of $W$.
583	Having said this, for any quasi-compact open $W \subset X$ we can
584	do induction on the number of affine opens needed to cover $W$
585	using the same trick as before and using that the quasi-compact open
586	$U_i \cap U_n$ is separated as an open subscheme of the affine scheme $U_n$.
587	\end{proof}
588	
589	\begin{lemma}
590	\label{lemma-vanishing-nr-affines}
591	\begin{slogan}
592	For schemes with affine diagonal, the cohomology of quasi-coherent
593	modules vanishes in degrees bigger than the number of affine
594	opens needed in a covering.
595	\end{slogan}
596	Let $X$ be a quasi-compact scheme with affine diagonal (for example
597	if $X$ is separated).
598	Let $t = t(X)$ be the minimal number of affine opens needed to
599	cover $X$. Then $H^n(X, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for all $n \geq t$ and all
600	quasi-coherent sheaves $\mathcal{F}$.
601	\end{lemma}
602	
603	\begin{proof}
604	First proof.
605	By induction on $t$.
606	If $t = 1$ the result follows from
607	Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}.
608	If $t > 1$ write $X = U \cup V$ with $V$ affine open and
609	$U = U_1 \cup \ldots \cup U_{t - 1}$ a union of $t - 1$ open affines.
610	Note that in this case
611	$U \cap V =  (U_1 \cap V) \cup \ldots (U_{t - 1} \cap V)$
612	is also a union of $t - 1$ affine open subschemes.
613	Namely, since the diagonal is affine, the intersection of two
614	affine opens is affine, see Lemma \ref{lemma-affine-diagonal}.
615	We apply the Mayer-Vietoris long exact sequence
616	$$
617	0 \to
618	H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \to
619	H^0(U, \mathcal{F}) \oplus H^0(V, \mathcal{F}) \to
620	H^0(U \cap V, \mathcal{F}) \to
621	H^1(X, \mathcal{F}) \to \ldots
622	$$
623	see Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-mayer-vietoris}.
624	By induction we see that the groups $H^i(U, \mathcal{F})$,
625	$H^i(V, \mathcal{F})$, $H^i(U \cap V, \mathcal{F})$ are zero for
626	$i \geq t - 1$. It follows immediately that $H^i(X, \mathcal{F})$
627	is zero for $i \geq t$.
628	
629	\medskip\noindent
630	Second proof.
631	Let $\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i = 1}^t U_i$ be a finite affine open
632	covering. Since $X$ is has affine diagonal the multiple intersections
633	$U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}$ are all affine, see
634	Lemma \ref{lemma-affine-diagonal}.
635	By Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent} the {\v C}ech
636	cohomology groups $\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
637	agree with the cohomology groups. By
638	Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-alternating-usual}
639	the {\v C}ech cohomology groups may be computed using the alternating
640	{\v C}ech complex $\check{\mathcal{C}}_{alt}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$.
641	As the covering consists of $t$ elements we see immediately
642	that $\check{\mathcal{C}}_{alt}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0$
643	for all $p \geq t$. Hence the result follows.
644	\end{proof}
645	
646	\begin{lemma}
647	\label{lemma-affine-diagonal-universal-delta-functor}
648	Let $X$ be a quasi-compact scheme with affine diagonal
649	(for example if $X$ is separated). Then
650	\begin{enumerate}
651	\item given a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{F}$
652	there exists an embedding $\mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{F}'$ of
653	quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
654	such that $H^p(X, \mathcal{F}') = 0$ for all $p \geq 1$, and
655	\item $\{H^n(X, -)\}_{n \geq 0}$
656	is a universal $\delta$-functor from $\QCoh(\mathcal{O}_X)$ to
657	$\textit{Ab}$.
658	\end{enumerate}
659	\end{lemma}
660	
661	\begin{proof}
662	Let $X = \bigcup U_i$ be a finite affine open covering.
663	Set $U = \coprod U_i$ and denote $j : U \to X$
664	the morphism inducing the given open immersions $U_i \to X$.
665	Since $U$ is an affine scheme and $X$ has affine diagonal,
666	the morphism $j$ is affine, see
667	Morphisms, Lemma \ref{morphisms-lemma-affine-permanence}.
668	For every $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{F}$ there is
669	a canonical map $\mathcal{F} \to j_*j^*\mathcal{F}$.
670	This map is injective as can be seen by checking on stalks:
671	if $x \in U_i$, then we have a factorization
672	$$
673	\mathcal{F}_x \to (j_*j^*\mathcal{F})_x
674	\to (j^*\mathcal{F})_{x'} = \mathcal{F}_x
675	$$
676	where $x' \in U$ is the point $x$ viewed as a point of $U_i \subset U$.
677	Now if $\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent, then $j^*\mathcal{F}$
678	is quasi-coherent on the affine scheme $U$ hence has vanishing
679	higher cohomology by
680	Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}.
681	Then $H^p(X, j_*j^*\mathcal{F}) = 0$ for
682	$p > 0$ by Lemma \ref{lemma-relative-affine-cohomology}
683	as $j$ is affine. This proves (1).
684	Finally, we see that the map
685	$H^p(X, \mathcal{F}) \to H^p(X, j_*j^*\mathcal{F})$
686	is zero and part (2) follows from
687	Homology, Lemma \ref{homology-lemma-efface-implies-universal}.
688	\end{proof}
689	
690	\begin{lemma}
691	\label{lemma-vanishing-nr-affines-quasi-separated}
692	Let $X$ be a quasi-compact quasi-separated scheme.
693	Let $X = U_1 \cup \ldots \cup U_n$ be an open covering
694	with each $U_i$ quasi-compact and separated (for example affine).
695	Set
696	$$
697	d = \max\nolimits_{I \subset \{1, \ldots, n\}}
698	\left(|I| + t(\bigcap\nolimits_{i \in I} U_i) - 1\right)
699	$$
700	where $t(U)$ is the minimal number of affines needed to cover
701	the scheme $U$. Then $H^p(X, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for all $p \geq d$ and all
702	quasi-coherent sheaves $\mathcal{F}$.
703	\end{lemma}
704	
705	\begin{proof}
706	Note that since $X$ is quasi-separated and $U_i$ quasi-compact the numbers
707	$t(\bigcap_{i \in I} U_i)$ are finite. Proof using induction on $n$.
708	If $n = 1$ then the result follows from Lemma \ref{lemma-vanishing-nr-affines}.
709	If $n > 1$, write $X = U \cup V$ with $U = U_1 \cup \ldots \cup U_{n - 1}$
710	and $V = U_n$. We apply the Mayer-Vietoris long exact sequence
711	$$
712	0 \to
713	H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \to
714	H^0(U, \mathcal{F}) \oplus H^0(V, \mathcal{F}) \to
715	H^0(U \cap V, \mathcal{F}) \to
716	H^1(X, \mathcal{F}) \to \ldots
717	$$
718	see Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-mayer-vietoris}.
719	To finish the proof for $q \geq d$ we will show that
720	$H^q(V, \mathcal{F})$, $H^q(U, \mathcal{F})$, and
721	$H^{q - 1}(U \cap V, \mathcal{F})$ vanish.
722	By the case $n = 1$ we have $H^q(V, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for
723	$q \geq t(V) = t(U_n)$. Since $t(V) \leq d$ this proves what we want.
724	By induction hypothesis we have $H^q(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for
725	$$
726	q \geq \max\nolimits_{I \subset \{1, \ldots, n - 1\}}
727	\left(|I| + t(\bigcap\nolimits_{i \in I} U_i) - 1\right)
728	$$
729	Since the integer on the right is less than or equal to $d$, this
730	proves what we want. Finally we may use our induction hypothesis for the open
731	$U \cap V = (U_1 \cap U_n) \cup \ldots \cup (U_{n - 1} \cap U_n)$ to get
732	the vanishing of $H^q(U \cap V, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for
733	$$
734	q \geq \max\nolimits_{I \subset \{1, \ldots, n - 1\}}
735	\left(|I| + t(U_n \cap \bigcap\nolimits_{i \in I} U_i) - 1\right)
736	$$
737	Since the integer on the right is strictly less than $d$
738	the lemma follows.
739	\end{proof}
740	
741	\begin{lemma}
742	\label{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images}
743	Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
744	Assume that $f$ is quasi-separated and quasi-compact.
745	\begin{enumerate}
746	\item For any quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{F}$ the
747	higher direct images $R^pf_*\mathcal{F}$ are quasi-coherent on $S$.
748	\item If $S$ is quasi-compact, there exists an integer $n = n(X, S, f)$
749	such that $R^pf_*\mathcal{F} = 0$ for all $p \geq n$ and any
750	quasi-coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ on $X$.
751	\item In fact, if $S$ is quasi-compact we can find $n = n(X, S, f)$
752	such that for every
753	morphism of schemes $S' \to S$ we have $R^p(f')_*\mathcal{F}' = 0$
754	for $p \geq n$ and any quasi-coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}'$
755	on $X'$. Here $f' : X' = S' \times_S X \to S'$ is the base change of $f$.
756	\end{enumerate}
757	\end{lemma}
758	
759	\begin{proof}
760	We first prove (1). Note that under the hypotheses of the lemma the sheaf
761	$R^0f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent by
762	Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-push-forward-quasi-coherent}.
763	Using
764	Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-localize-higher-direct-images}
765	we see that forming higher direct images commutes with restriction
766	to open subschemes. Since being quasi-coherent is local on $S$ we
767	reduce to the case discussed in the next paragraph.
768	
769	\medskip\noindent
770	Proof of (1) in case $S$ is affine. We will use the induction principle.
771	Since $f$ quasi-compact and quasi-separated we see that $X$
772	is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. For $U \subset X$
773	quasi-compact open and $a = f|_U$ we let $P(U)$ be the property that
774	$R^pa_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent on $S$ for all quasi-coherent modules
775	$\mathcal{F}$ on $U$ and all $p \geq 0$. Since $P(X)$ is (1), it suffices
776	the prove conditions (1) and (2) of Lemma \ref{lemma-induction-principle} hold.
777	If $U$ is affine, then $P(U)$ holds because $R^pa_*\mathcal{F} = 0$ for
778	$p \geq 1$ (by  Lemma \ref{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing} and
779	Morphisms, Lemma \ref{morphisms-lemma-morphism-affines-affine})
780	and we've already observed the result holds for $p = 0$ in the first
781	paragraph. Next, let $U \subset X$ be a quasi-compact open, $V \subset X$
782	an affine open, and assume $P(U)$, $P(V)$, $P(U \cap V)$ hold.
783	Let $a = f|_U$, $b = f|_V$, $c = f|_{U \cap V}$, and $g = f|_{U \cup V}$.
784	Then for any quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_{U \cup V}$-module $\mathcal{F}$
785	we have the relative Mayer-Vietoris sequence
786	$$
787	0 \to
788	g_*\mathcal{F} \to
789	a_*(\mathcal{F}|_U) \oplus b_*(\mathcal{F}|_V) \to
790	c_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U \cap V}) \to
791	R^1g_*\mathcal{F} \to \ldots
792	$$
793	see Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-relative-mayer-vietoris}.
794	By $P(U)$, $P(V)$, $P(U \cap V)$ we see that
795	$R^pa_*(\mathcal{F}|_U)$, $R^pb_*(\mathcal{F}|_V)$ and
796	$R^pc_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U \cap V})$ are all quasi-coherent.
797	Using the results on quasi-coherent sheaves in
798	Schemes, Section \ref{schemes-section-quasi-coherent}
799	this implies that each of the sheaves
800	$R^pg_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent since it sits in the middle of a short
801	exact sequence with a cokernel of a map between quasi-coherent sheaves
802	on the left and a kernel of a map between quasi-coherent sheaves on the right.
803	Whence $P(U \cup V)$ and the proof of (1) is complete.
804	
805	\medskip\noindent
806	Next, we prove (3) and a fortiori (2). Choose a finite affine open
807	covering $S = \bigcup_{j = 1, \ldots m} S_j$. For each $j$ choose
808	a finite affine open covering
809	$f^{-1}(S_j) = \bigcup_{i = 1, \ldots t_j} U_{ji} $.
810	Let
811	$$
812	d_j = \max\nolimits_{I \subset \{1, \ldots, t_j\}}
813	\left(|I| + t(\bigcap\nolimits_{i \in I} U_{ji})\right)
814	$$
815	be the integer found in
816	Lemma \ref{lemma-vanishing-nr-affines-quasi-separated}.
817	We claim that $n(X, S, f) = \max d_j$ works.
818	
819	\medskip\noindent
820	Namely, let $S' \to S$ be a morphism of schemes and let
821	$\mathcal{F}'$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X' = S' \times_S X$.
822	We want to show that $R^pf'_*\mathcal{F}' = 0$ for $p \geq n(X, S, f)$.
823	Since this question is local on $S'$ we may assume that $S'$ is affine
824	and maps into $S_j$ for some $j$. Then $X' = S' \times_{S_j} f^{-1}(S_j)$
825	is covered by the open affines $S' \times_{S_j} U_{ji}$, $i = 1, \ldots t_j$
826	and the intersections
827	$$
828	\bigcap\nolimits_{i \in I} S' \times_{S_j} U_{ji} =
829	S' \times_{S_j} \bigcap\nolimits_{i \in I} U_{ji}
830	$$
831	are covered by the same number of affines as before the base change.
832	Applying
833	Lemma \ref{lemma-vanishing-nr-affines-quasi-separated}
834	we get $H^p(X', \mathcal{F}') = 0$. By the first part of the proof
835	we already know that each $R^qf'_*\mathcal{F}'$ is quasi-coherent
836	hence has vanishing higher cohomology groups on our affine scheme $S'$,
837	thus we see that $H^0(S', R^pf'_*\mathcal{F}') = H^p(X', \mathcal{F}') = 0$
838	by Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-apply-Leray}.
839	Since $R^pf'_*\mathcal{F}'$ is quasi-coherent
840	we conclude that $R^pf'_*\mathcal{F}' = 0$.
841	\end{proof}
842	
843	\begin{lemma}
844	\label{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}
845	Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
846	Assume that $f$ is quasi-separated and quasi-compact.
847	Assume $S$ is affine.
848	For any quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{F}$
849	we have
850	$$
851	H^q(X, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^qf_*\mathcal{F})
852	$$
853	for all $q \in \mathbf{Z}$.
854	\end{lemma}
855	
856	\begin{proof}
857	Consider the Leray spectral sequence $E_2^{p, q} = H^p(S, R^qf_*\mathcal{F})$
858	converging to $H^{p + q}(X, \mathcal{F})$, see
859	Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-Leray}.
860	By Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images}
861	we see that the sheaves $R^qf_*\mathcal{F}$ are quasi-coherent.
862	By Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}
863	we see that $E_2^{p, q} = 0$ when $p > 0$.
864	Hence the spectral sequence degenerates at $E_2$ and we win.
865	See also
866	Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-apply-Leray} (2)
867	for the general principle.
868	\end{proof}
869	
870	
871	
872	
873	
874	
875	
876	
877	\section{Cohomology and base change, I}
878	\label{section-cohomology-and-base-change}
879	
880	\noindent
881	Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
882	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X$.
883	Suppose further that $g : S' \to S$ is any morphism of schemes. Denote
884	$X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
Now the base change section:

884	$X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
885	$f' : X' \to S'$ the base change of $f$.
886	Also write $g' : X' \to X$ the projection,
887	and set $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$.
888	Here is a diagram representing the situation:
889	\begin{equation}
890	\label{equation-base-change-diagram}
891	\vcenter{
892	\xymatrix{
893	\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F} &
894	X' \ar[r]_{g'} \ar[d]_{f'} &
895	X \ar[d]^f &
896	\mathcal{F} \\
897	Rf'_*\mathcal{F}' &
898	S' \ar[r]^g &
899	S &
900	Rf_*\mathcal{F}
901	}
902	}
903	\end{equation}
904	Here is the simplest case of the base change property we have in mind.
905	
906	\begin{lemma}
907	\label{lemma-affine-base-change}
908	Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
909	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
910	Assume $f$ is affine.
911	In this case $f_*\mathcal{F} \cong Rf_*\mathcal{F}$ is
912	a quasi-coherent sheaf, and for every base change diagram
913	(\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
914	we have
915	$$
916	g^*f_*\mathcal{F} = f'_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}.
917	$$
918	\end{lemma}
919	
920	\begin{proof}
921	The vanishing of higher direct images is
922	Lemma \ref{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing}.
923	The statement is local on $S$ and $S'$. Hence we may
924	assume $X = \Spec(A)$, $S = \Spec(R)$,
925	$S' = \Spec(R')$ and $\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}$
926	for some $A$-module $M$.
927	We use Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-widetilde-pullback}
928	to describe pullbacks and pushforwards of $\mathcal{F}$.
929	Namely, $X' = \Spec(R' \otimes_R A)$ and
930	$\mathcal{F}'$ is the quasi-coherent sheaf associated
931	to $(R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M$.
932	Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
933	equality
934	$$
935	(R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
936	$$
937	as $R'$-modules.
938	\end{proof}
939	
940	\noindent
941	In many situations it is sufficient to know about the following
942	special case of cohomology and base change. It follows immediately
943	from the stronger results in
944	Section \ref{section-cohomology-and-base-change-derived},
945	but since it is so important it deserves its own proof.
946	
947	\begin{lemma}[Flat base change]
948	\label{lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology}
949	Consider a cartesian diagram of schemes
950	$$
951	\xymatrix{
952	X' \ar[d]_{f'} \ar[r]_{g'} & X \ar[d]^f \\
953	S' \ar[r]^g & S
954	}
955	$$
956	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
957	with pullback $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$.
958	Assume that $g$ is flat and that $f$ is quasi-compact and quasi-separated.
959	For any $i \geq 0$
960	\begin{enumerate}
961	\item the base change map of
962	Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-base-change-map-flat-case}
963	is an isomorphism
964	$$
965	g^*R^if_*\mathcal{F} \longrightarrow R^if'_*\mathcal{F}',
966	$$
967	\item if $S = \Spec(A)$ and $S' = \Spec(B)$, then
968	$H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$.
969	\end{enumerate}
970	\end{lemma}
971	
972	\begin{proof}
973	Using Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-base-change-map-flat-case} in (1)
974	is allowed since $g'$ is flat by
975	Morphisms, Lemma \ref{morphisms-lemma-base-change-flat}.
976	Having said this, part (1) follows from part (2). Namely,
977	part (1) is local on $S'$ and hence we may assume $S$
978	and $S'$ are affine. In other words, we have $S = \Spec(A)$
979	and $S' = \Spec(B)$ as in (2).
980	Then since $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent
981	(Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images}),
982	it is the quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_S$-module associated to the
983	$A$-module $H^0(S, R^if_*\mathcal{F}) = H^i(X, \mathcal{F})$
984	(equality by
985	Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}).
986	Similarly, $R^if'_*\mathcal{F}'$ is the quasi-coherent
987	$\mathcal{O}_{S'}$-module associated to the $B$-module
988	$H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$. Since pullback by $g$ corresponds
989	to $- \otimes_A B$ on modules
990	(Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-widetilde-pullback})
991	we see that it suffices to prove (2).
992	
993	\medskip\noindent
994	Let $A \to B$ be a flat ring homomorphism.
995	Let $X$ be a quasi-compact and quasi-separated scheme over $A$.
996	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
997	Set $X_B = X \times_{\Spec(A)} \Spec(B)$ and denote
998	$\mathcal{F}_B$ the pullback of $\mathcal{F}$.
999	We are trying to show that the map
1000	$$
1001	H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow H^i(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
1002	$$
1003	(given by the reference in the statement of the lemma)
1004	is an isomorphism.
1005	
1006	\medskip\noindent
1007	In case $X$ is separated, choose an affine open covering
1008	$\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \ldots \cup U_t$ and recall that
1009	$$
1010	\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F}),
1011	$$
1012	see
1013	Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent}.
1014	If $\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \ldots \cup (U_t)_B$ we obtain
1015	by base change, then it is still the case that each $(U_i)_B$ is affine
1016	and that $X_B$ is separated. Thus we obtain
1017	$$
1018	\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) = H^p(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B).
1019	$$
1020	We have the following relation between the {\v C}ech complexes
1021	$$
1022	\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) =
1023	\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B
1024	$$
1025	as follows from
1026	Lemma \ref{lemma-affine-base-change}.
1027	Since $A \to B$ is flat, the same thing remains true on taking cohomology.
1028	
1029	\medskip\noindent
1030	In case $X$ is quasi-separated, choose an affine open covering
1031	$\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \ldots \cup U_t$. We will use the
1032	{\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence
1033	Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-spectral-sequence}.
1034	The reader who wishes to avoid this spectral sequence
1035	can use Mayer-Vietoris and induction on $t$ as in the proof of
1036	Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images}.
1037	The spectral sequence has $E_2$-page
1038	$E_2^{p, q} = \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}))$
1039	and converges to $H^{p + q}(X, \mathcal{F})$.
1040	Similarly, we have a spectral sequence with $E_2$-page
1041	$E_2^{p, q} = \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}_B))$
1042	which converges to $H^{p + q}(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)$.
1043	Since the intersections $U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}$ are quasi-compact
1044	and separated, the result of the second paragraph of the proof gives
1045	$\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}_B)) =
1046	\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F})) \otimes_A B$.
1047	Using that $A \to B$ is flat we conclude that
1048	$H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \to H^i(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)$
1049	is an isomorphism for all $i$ and we win.
1050	\end{proof}
1051	
1052	\begin{lemma}[Finite locally free base change]
1053	\label{lemma-finite-locally-free-base-change-cohomology}
1054	Consider a cartesian diagram of schemes
1055	$$
1056	\xymatrix{
1057	Y \ar[d]_{g} \ar[r]_h & X \ar[d]^f \\
1058	\Spec(B) \ar[r] & \Spec(A)
1059	}
1060	$$
1061	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
1062	with pullback $\mathcal{G} = h^*\mathcal{F}$.
1063	If $B$ is a finite locally free $A$-module, then
1064	$H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(Y, \mathcal{G})$.
1065	\end{lemma}
1066	
1067	\noindent
1068	{\bf Warning}: Do not use this lemma unless you understand the difference
1069	between this and Lemma \ref{lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology}.
1070	
1071	\begin{proof}
1072	In case $X$ is separated, choose an affine open covering
1073	$\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$ and recall that
1074	$$
1075	\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F}),
1076	$$
1077	see
1078	Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent}.
1079	Let $\mathcal{V} : Y = \bigcup_{i \in I} g^{-1}(U_i)$
1080	be the corresponding affine open covering of $Y$.
1081	The opens $V_i = g^{-1}(U_i) = U_i \times_{\Spec(A)} \Spec(B)$
1082	are affine and $Y$ is separated. Thus we obtain
1083	$$

1	\chapter{Higher direct images $R^i f_*$ of quasi-coherent sheaves ($i \geq 1$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/HigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tags 02KE
5	% (Quasi-coherence of higher direct images), 02KG (relative affine vanishing),
6	% and 02KH (Flat base change) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
7	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
8	
9	\section{Setup and motivation}
10	
11	This chapter treats the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
12	\(i \geq 1\), complementing the \(i = 0\) (direct-image) case of
13	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}. These higher direct images are the
14	single deepest foundation of the cohomology-and-base-change package: the
15	Cohen--Macaulay regularity bound, semicontinuity of fibre cohomology, and the
16	\(m\)-regularity estimate of FGA all rest on the basic finiteness and base-change
17	properties of \(R^i f_*\) recorded here.
18	
19	We use the classical cohomology-sheaf formulation (not the derived category) and a
20	{\v C}ech-complex approach, parallel to the \(i = 0\) treatment of
21	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}.
22	
23	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
24	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
25	A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded by the
26	cartesian square obtained from the fibre product \(X' = X \times_S S'\):
27	\[
28	  \begin{array}{ccc}
29	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
30	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
31	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
32	  \end{array}
33	\]
34	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
35	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
36	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
37	
38	\section{Definition of the higher direct images}
39	
40	\begin{definition}\leanok
41	[Higher direct image]
42	  \label{def:higher_direct_image}
43	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage}
44	  % NOTE (iter-233): the Lean `higherDirectImage` carries an explicit instance
45	  % hypothesis `[HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]`, because Mathlib has
46	  % `Abelian X.Modules` but NOT `EnoughInjectives` / `IsGrothendieckAbelian` for
47	  % `SheafOfModules` over a sheaf of rings (the Grothendieck-abelian instance is
48	  % only for `Sheaf J A` with a fixed value category). The definition is therefore
49	  % conditional; the hypothesis can be dropped once a lane proves that instance.
50	  % The file is not yet imported by the aggregator (orphan module).
51	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
52	  % `lemma-relative-affine-vanishing' (proof), describing $R^i f_*$ as the
53	  % $i$-th right derived functor of $f_*$ via the sheafified presheaf

\chapter{{\v C}ech computation of higher direct images $R^i f_*$ (unconditional)}
\label{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage}
% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechHigherDirectImage.lean
% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
% quasi-coherent sheaves and \S Quasi-coherence of higher direct images
% (Lemmas \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial},
% \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero},
% \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent},
% \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application},
% \ref{lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology}) (read from
% references/stacks-coherent.tex).

\section{Setup and motivation}

This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
derived functor, which in a formalisation requires the ambient category of
\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives; that property is not currently
available for sheaves of modules over a sheaf of rings whose value category varies
over the site. The {\v C}ech approach developed here sidesteps the issue entirely:
it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).

Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
\[
  \begin{array}{ccc}
    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
  \end{array}
\]
Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).

\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}

\begin{definition}
[{\v C}ech nerve of an affine open cover]
  \label{def:cech_nerve}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechNerve}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
  % quasi-coherent sheaves, opening paragraph and Lemma
  % \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}
  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L36--67).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
  % Let $U \subset X$ be an affine open.
  % Recall that a {\it standard open covering} of $U$ is a covering
  % of the form $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$
  % where $f_1, \ldots, f_n \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ generate
  % the unit ideal, see
  % Schemes, Definition \ref{schemes-definition-standard-covering}."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
  quasi-coherent sheaves.}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite open cover of \(X\) by
  affine opens, with index set \(I\). For a finite nonempty tuple
  \(i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I\) write
  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = U_{i_0} \cap \cdots \cap U_{i_p}\) for the corresponding
  intersection. The \emph{{\v C}ech nerve} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the augmented
  simplicial object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) whose object in simplicial degree
  \(p\) is the direct image, along the open immersions
  \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\), of the restriction
  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (or of any chosen quasi-coherent sheaf) to the
  \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
  \[
    [p] \;\longmapsto\;
      \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
        (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_* \,\bigl(\,\cdot\,|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
  \]
  with faces given by the restriction maps that omit one index and degeneracies by
  the maps that repeat one index; the augmentation in degree \(-1\) is the object
  on all of \(X\). When \(X\) is separated each intersection
  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is itself affine. The local affine model is the
  \emph{standard open covering} \(U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) of an affine open
  \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) by basic opens, where \(f_1, \ldots, f_n \in A\)
  generate the unit ideal; on such a cover the intersections are again basic opens
  \(D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\).
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}
[{\v C}ech complex of a quasi-coherent sheaf]
  \label{def:cech_complex}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechComplex}
  \uses{def:cech_nerve}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
  % \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof)
  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L59--77).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
  % is identified with the complex
  % $$
  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
  % \ldots
  % $$
  % We are asked to show that the extended complex
  % \begin{equation}
  % \label{equation-extended}
  % 0 \to
  % M \to
  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
  % \ldots
  % \end{equation}
  % (whose truncation we have studied in
  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is exact."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
  \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}.}
  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and let
  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover with
  all intersections \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) affine (e.g.\ \(X\) separated). The
  \emph{{\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
  is the cochain complex obtained by applying the global-sections functor to the
  {\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} evaluated at \(\mathcal{F}\);
  in degree \(p\) it is
  \[
    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
      = \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
  \]
  with differential \(d : \check{\mathcal{C}}^p \to \check{\mathcal{C}}^{p+1}\) the
  alternating sum \((d s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\,
  s_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}}\) of the
  restriction maps. Over an affine \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) with
  \(\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}\) and a standard cover by the
  \(D(f_i)\), this is the complex
  \(\prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \to \cdots\) of
  localisations. The \emph{relative {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet
  (\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) for \(f : X \to S\) is the analogous complex in
  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) obtained by applying \(f_*\) over each finite
  intersection instead of global sections; its terms are
  \(\prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} (f|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})_*
  \bigl(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr)\), and it is a complex in
  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) because each \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine and the
  pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf along a quasi-compact quasi-separated
  morphism is quasi-coherent.
\end{definition}

\section{Affine acyclicity of the {\v C}ech complex}

\begin{lemma}
[{\v C}ech acyclicity on affines]
  \label{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechAcyclic.affine}
  \uses{def:cech_complex}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KG (affine vanishing);

  % {\v C}ech vanishing) and Lemma
  % \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero} (Serre vanishing on affines)
  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L44--52 and L145--155).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
  % Let $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$ be a standard
  % open covering of an affine open of $X$.
  % Then $\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for
  % all $p > 0$."
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Serre vanishing: Higher cohomology vanishes on affine schemes
  % for quasi-coherent modules.
  % Let $X$ be a scheme.
  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
  % For any affine open $U \subset X$ we have
  % $H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for all $p > 0$."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemmas
  \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} and
  \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero} (Serre vanishing on affines;
  Tag 02KG).}
  Let \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) be an affine scheme, \(\mathcal{F}\) a
  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module, and
  \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) a standard open cover (so the
  \(f_i \in A\) generate the unit ideal). Then the {\v C}ech complex
  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) has vanishing cohomology
  in all positive degrees:
  \[
    \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } p > 0,
  \]
  and consequently \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\).
\end{lemma}

% Choose and fix such an element $i_{\text{fix}}$. ...

% M_{f_{i_0} \ldots f_{i_{p + 1}}, \mathfrak p}
% \longrightarrow

% $$
% by the rule
% $$
% h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_{\text{fix}} i_0 \ldots i_p}
% $$
% ... We compute ... $(dh + hd)(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_0 \ldots i_p}$ ...
% This proves the identity map is homotopic to zero as desired."
% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We are going to apply
% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-vanish-basis}.
% As our basis $\mathcal{B}$ for the topology of $X$ we are going to use
% the affine opens of $X$. ... Note that the intersection of standard opens
% in an affine is another standard open. Hence property (1) holds. ...
% Finally, condition (3) of the lemma follows from
% Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}."
\begin{proof}

  \(A\)-module \(M\). By Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} the {\v C}ech complex of
  the standard cover is the complex of localisations
  \[
    \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \;\to\;
    \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \;\to\;
    \prod_{i_0 i_1 i_2} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1} f_{i_2}} \;\to\; \cdots,
  \]
  and the claim \(\check{H}^p = 0\) for \(p > 0\) is equivalent to exactness of the
  extended complex \(0 \to M \to \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \cdots\). Exactness may
  be checked after localising at an arbitrary prime \(\mathfrak{p} \subset A\).

  \(f_{i_{\mathrm{fix}}} \notin \mathfrak{p}\); then
  \(M_{f_{i_{\mathrm{fix}}}, \mathfrak{p}} = M_{\mathfrak{p}}\), and the
  prescription \(h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_{\mathrm{fix}} i_0 \ldots i_p}\)
  defines a contracting homotopy of the localised extended complex: a direct
  computation gives \((dh + hd)(s) = s\), so the identity is homotopic to zero and
  the localised complex is exact. As exactness holds after every localisation, the
  extended complex is exact and \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for

  the topology and the standard open covers as the admissible covers. Intersections

  vanishing for quasi-coherent sheaves on affine schemes.)}

\section{The {\v C}ech complex computes $R^i f_*$}

\begin{lemma}
[{\v C}ech complex computes the higher direct images]
  \label{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cech_computes_higherDirectImage}
  \uses{lem:cech_acyclic_affine, def:cech_complex}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KE; Lemma
  % \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent} ({\v C}ech computes cohomology when
  % all intersections are affine) and Lemma
  % \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}
  % ($H^q(X,\mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^q f_*\mathcal{F})$ for affine $S$)
  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L245--256 and L843--854).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
  % Let $\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$ be an open covering such that
  % $U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}$ is affine open for all $p \ge 0$ and all
  % $i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I$.
  % In this case for any quasi-coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ we have
  % $$
  % \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F})
  % $$
  % as $\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$-modules for all $p$."
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
  % Assume that $f$ is quasi-separated and quasi-compact.
  % Assume $S$ is affine.
  % For any quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{F}$
  % we have
  % $$
  % H^q(X, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^qf_*\mathcal{F})
  % $$
  % for all $q \in \mathbf{Z}$."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemmas
  \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent} and
  \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application} (Tag 02KE).}
  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a separated quasi-compact morphism of schemes, let
  \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and let
  \(\mathfrak{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover of
  \(X\) (so, by separatedness, every intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is
  affine). Then the cohomology sheaves of the relative {\v C}ech complex
  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
  Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} compute the higher direct images: for every
  \(i \geq 0\) there is a canonical isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules
  \[
    H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr)
      \;\cong\; R^i f_*\mathcal{F}.
  \]
  In particular, over an affine base \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), taking global
  sections gives \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = \check{H}^i(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) =
  H^0(S, R^i f_*\mathcal{F})\) as \(A\)-modules for all \(i\).
\end{lemma}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "In view of
% Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}
% this is a special case of
% Cohomology, Lemma
% \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-spectral-sequence-application}."
% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Consider the Leray spectral sequence
% $E_2^{p, q} = H^p(S, R^qf_*\mathcal{F})$
% converging to $H^{p + q}(X, \mathcal{F})$, see
% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-Leray}.
% By Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images}
% we see that the sheaves $R^qf_*\mathcal{F}$ are quasi-coherent.
% By Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}
% we see that $E_2^{p, q} = 0$ when $p > 0$.
% Hence the spectral sequence degenerates at $E_2$ and we win."
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:cech_acyclic_affine, def:cech_complex}
  The question is local on \(S\), and the formation of both sides commutes with
  restriction to opens of \(S\), so we may assume \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) is
  affine. Because \(f\) is separated and quasi-compact, each finite intersection
  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine, and \(f\) restricted to it is an affine
  morphism; by the affine acyclicity of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} the
  higher direct images of \(\mathcal{F}\) over each such intersection vanish, so the
  terms of the relative {\v C}ech complex carry no hidden higher cohomology.

  Consider the {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence of the cover
  \(\mathfrak{U}\), with \(E_2\)-page
  \(E_2^{p,q} = \check{H}^p(\mathfrak{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}))\)
  converging to \(H^{p+q}(X, \mathcal{F})\). Since all intersections are affine,
  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} forces the presheaf cohomology
  \(\underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F})\) to vanish on the intersections for \(q > 0\), so
  the spectral sequence collapses to its row \(q = 0\): the {\v C}ech cohomology of
  \(\mathcal{F}\) for the cover \(\mathfrak{U}\) computes the sheaf cohomology,
  \(\check{H}^p(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F})\) for all \(p\),
  and the same identification holds relatively, i.e.\ the cohomology sheaves of
  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) compute the cohomology
  sheaves of \(Rf_*\mathcal{F}\).

  It remains to identify these cohomology sheaves with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\). Over
  the affine base \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), the Leray spectral sequence
  \(E_2^{p,q} = H^p(S, R^q f_*\mathcal{F}) \Rightarrow H^{p+q}(X, \mathcal{F})\)
  has \(R^q f_*\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent, whence \(H^p(S, R^q f_*\mathcal{F}) = 0\)
  for \(p > 0\) by Serre vanishing (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}). The Leray
  sequence therefore degenerates at \(E_2\), giving
  \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^i f_*\mathcal{F})\). Combining with the previous
  paragraph, \(H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr)
  \cong R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules for all \(i \geq 0\).
\end{proof}

\section{The unconditional higher direct image}

\begin{definition}
[Unconditional higher direct image via {\v C}ech]
  \label{def:cech_higher_direct_image}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechHigherDirectImage}
  \uses{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
  % \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}
  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L843--854); the unconditional
  % packaging via the {\v C}ech complex is an Archon-original construction grounded
  % in this lemma and Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent}.
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
  % Assume that $f$ is quasi-separated and quasi-compact.
  % Assume $S$ is affine.
  % For any quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{F}$
  % we have
  % $$
  % H^q(X, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^qf_*\mathcal{F})
  % $$
  % for all $q \in \mathbf{Z}$."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
  \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}; unconditional
  {\v C}ech packaging is Archon-original.}
  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a separated quasi-compact morphism and \(\mathcal{F}\) a
  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Fix a finite affine open cover
  \(\mathfrak{U}\) of \(X\). The \emph{(unconditional) \(i\)-th higher direct
  image} is defined, for each \(i \geq 0\), as the \(i\)-th cohomology sheaf of the
  relative {\v C}ech complex,
  \[
    R^i f_*\mathcal{F} \;:=\;
      H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr)
      \in \operatorname{QCoh}(S).
  \]
  This requires \emph{no} hypothesis that the category of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
  have enough injectives: the right-hand side is the cohomology of an explicit
  complex of quasi-coherent sheaves. By
  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_computes_cohomology} the result is canonically isomorphic to
  the derived-functor higher direct image wherever the latter is defined, and in
  particular is independent of the chosen affine cover \(\mathfrak{U}\) up to
  canonical isomorphism: two affine covers admit a common affine refinement, and
  the induced refinement maps on {\v C}ech complexes are quasi-isomorphisms because
  both compute the same \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) by
  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}. For \(i = 0\) one recovers the ordinary
  pushforward \(R^0 f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*\mathcal{F}\).
\end{definition}

\section{Flat base change for the {\v C}ech higher direct images}

\begin{lemma}
[Flat base change of $R^i f_*$ via {\v C}ech]
  \label{lem:cech_flat_base_change}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cech_flatBaseChange}
  \uses{def:cech_higher_direct_image, lem:cech_computes_cohomology}

  % \ref{lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology}, "Flat base change")
  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L947--970).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Consider a cartesian diagram of schemes
  % $$
  % \xymatrix{
  % X' \ar[d]_{f'} \ar[r]_{g'} & X \ar[d]^f \\

  % }
  % $$
  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
  % with pullback $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$.
  % Assume that $g$ is flat and that $f$ is quasi-compact and quasi-separated.

  % is an isomorphism
  % $$
  % g^*R^if_*\mathcal{F} \longrightarrow R^if'_*\mathcal{F}',
  % $$
  % \item if $S = \Spec(A)$ and $S' = \Spec(B)$, then
  % $H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$.
  % \end{enumerate}"
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
  \ref{lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology} (Tag 02KH, ``Flat base change'').}
  Consider the cartesian square
  \[
    \begin{array}{ccc}
      X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
      {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
      S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
    \end{array}
  \]
  with \(f : X \to S\) separated and quasi-compact, \(\mathcal{F}\) a
  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and
  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). Assume \(g\) is flat. Then for every
  \(i \geq 0\) the canonical base-change map between the unconditional {\v C}ech
  higher direct images of Definition~\ref{def:cech_higher_direct_image} is an
  isomorphism
  \[
    g^*\bigl(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
      R^i f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr) = R^i f'_*\mathcal{F}'.
  \]
  Equivalently, when \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and
  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the comparison map of
  \(B\)-modules \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \to H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')\) is an
  isomorphism.
\end{lemma}

% and $S'$ are affine. ...
% In case $X$ is separated, choose an affine open covering
% $\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \ldots \cup U_t$ and recall that
% $$
% \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F}),
% $$
% see
% Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent}.
% If $\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \ldots \cup (U_t)_B$ we obtain
% by base change, then it is still the case that each $(U_i)_B$ is affine
% and that $X_B$ is separated. ...
% We have the following relation between the {\v C}ech complexes
% $$
% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) =
% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B
% $$
% as follows from
% Lemma \ref{lemma-affine-base-change}.
% Since $A \to B$ is flat, the same thing remains true on taking cohomology."
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:cech_higher_direct_image, lem:cech_computes_cohomology}
  The assertion that the base-change map is an isomorphism is local on \(S'\), so we
  may assume \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with
  \(A \to B\) flat. By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_computes_cohomology} both
  \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) and \(R^i f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) are quasi-coherent, associated
  to the \(A\)-module \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F})\) and the \(B\)-module
  \(H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')\) respectively; since pullback along \(g\) corresponds to
  \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules, it suffices to prove that the comparison map
  \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \to H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')\) is an isomorphism.

  Choose a finite affine open cover \(\mathfrak{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\);
  by separatedness all intersections \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) are affine. Base
  changing the cover yields an affine open cover \(\mathfrak{U}_B\) of the
  again-separated \(X' = X_B\), with each \((U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_B\) affine. By
  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_computes_cohomology} the two {\v C}ech complexes compute the
  respective cohomologies. The affine base change for the \(i = 0\) direct image
  identifies each term of the base-changed {\v C}ech complex with the original term
  tensored over \(A\) with \(B\), giving an isomorphism of cochain complexes
  \[
    \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
      \;\cong\;
      \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B.
  \]
  Because \(A \to B\) is flat, \(- \otimes_A B\) is exact and commutes with the
  formation of cohomology of a complex; applying \(H^i\) for any \(i \geq 0\) yields
  \[
    H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
      H^i(X', \mathcal{F}').
  \]
  Translating back to sheaves on \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) — using that the
  unconditional higher direct images are the quasi-coherent sheaves associated to
  these modules — gives the asserted isomorphism
  \(g^*(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}) \xrightarrow{\sim} R^i f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) for all
  \(i \geq 0\). The flatness of \(g\) (hence of \(g'\), by base change) is exactly
  what makes \(- \otimes_A B\) exact, and is therefore the precise hypothesis under
  which the {\v C}ech computation of Definition~\ref{def:cech_higher_direct_image}
  is compatible with base change.
\end{proof}
